skill.name: azure-kubernetes
skill.description: Plan, create, and configure production-ready Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) clusters. Covers Day-0 checklist, SKU selection (Automatic vs Standard), networking options (private API server, Azure CNI Overlay, egress configuration), security, and operations (autoscaling, upgrade strategy, cost analysis). WHEN: create AKS environment, provision AKS environment, enable AKS observability, design AKS networking, choose AKS SKU, secure AKS.
skill.summary: Plan secure Azure Kubernetes Service clusters with networking, identity, observability, upgrades, and cost controls.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command and weak-crypto patterns, but the reviewed files contain documentation, not executable code. The weak cryptography and hardcoded URL alerts are false positives. The residual risk is that users may run Azure CLI or MCP actions that change AKS resources, credentials, and cloud costs.

medium_findings.title: Azure Commands Can Modify Cloud Resources
medium_findings.description: The skill documents Azure CLI and MCP AKS operations. These commands are legitimate, but they can create clusters, change credentials, enable monitoring, or affect costs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files clearly reference AKS CLI and MCP actions that can mutate Azure resources. The context is operational guidance, not hidden execution or malware.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography matches occur in normal AKS guidance text. I found no code that selects DES, MD5, RC4, SHA1, or another weak algorithm.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review showed prose and command names only, with no cryptographic implementation or configuration. The repeated line matches align with substring noise.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Alerts Are Legitimate Azure Inventory Commands
low_findings.description: The list and show commands inspect AKS resources. They can reveal operational metadata, but they are expected for cluster planning and troubleshooting.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are normal Azure inventory and context checks. Risk remains if a user shares outputs containing subscription or cluster details.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Is Benign Documentation
low_findings.description: The only hardcoded URL points to Microsoft Learn documentation for Azure CNI Overlay. It is not an exfiltration endpoint or runtime network call.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is a plain documentation reference under learn.microsoft.com. No file contains code that sends data to this URL.

content.value_statement: AKS design choices can be hard to change after cluster creation. This skill guides secure planning for networking, identity, observability, upgrades, and cost controls.
actual_capabilities: Compares AKS Automatic and AKS Standard for user requirements.
Guides Day-0 networking choices, including API access, pod IP model, ingress, and egress.
Recommends identity, secret management, policy, and workload security settings.
Plans observability with Container Insights, Managed Prometheus, Grafana, and diagnostic settings.
Suggests upgrade, patching, node pool, reliability, and cost-control practices.
Provides Azure CLI examples for common AKS planning and provisioning tasks.
limitations: It does not execute Azure commands by itself.
It depends on current Azure permissions, quotas, regions, and subscription policy.
It cannot guarantee zero downtime for upgrades or cluster changes.
It provides guidance, not a substitute for production architecture review.

use_cases.title: Design a New Production Cluster
use_cases.description: Create a checklist for AKS SKU, networking, identity, observability, upgrades, and reliability before provisioning.

use_cases.title: Choose AKS Networking Defaults
use_cases.description: Compare Azure CNI Overlay, VNet-routable pods, egress controls, ingress options, and DNS settings.

use_cases.title: Prepare Operations Guardrails
use_cases.description: Define monitoring, policy, maintenance windows, node pools, autoscaling, and cost practices for existing AKS plans.

prompt_templates.title: Start an AKS Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a dev AKS cluster. Ask only for required inputs, then recommend safe defaults.

prompt_templates.title: Compare AKS SKUs
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare AKS Automatic and AKS Standard for my workload, including control, operations, cost, and networking tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Design Secure Networking
prompt_templates.prompt: Design AKS networking for a private production cluster with restricted egress, ingress, DNS, and pod IP requirements.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Production Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my proposed AKS configuration for Day-0 risks, security gaps, upgrade strategy, observability, reliability, and cost controls.

output_examples.input: I need a production AKS cluster for web services with private networking.
output: Recommended SKU: AKS Automatic unless custom node pool control is required.
Networking: private API server, Azure CNI Overlay, restricted egress through firewall, and Gateway API ingress.
Security: Microsoft Entra ID, Workload Identity, Key Vault CSI Driver, Azure Policy, and signed images.
Operations: Managed Prometheus, Container Insights, maintenance windows, auto-upgrades, and availability zones.

output_examples.input: Should I use Azure CNI Overlay or VNet-routable pod IPs?
output: Use Azure CNI Overlay for most clusters because it scales well and conserves VNet address space.
Use VNet-routable pod IPs only when pods must be directly reachable from the VNet or on-premises networks.
Treat this as a Day-0 decision because changing the pod IP model often requires cluster recreation.

output_examples.input: Review my AKS operations plan before launch.
output: Add maintenance windows and staged upgrades before production traffic.
Enable control plane logs, audit logs, Managed Prometheus, and Grafana dashboards.
Use PodDisruptionBudgets, probes, replicas, topology spread constraints, and separate system node pools.
Confirm regional quota, cost controls, and node SKU choices before provisioning.

best_practices: Decide networking, API access, and pod IP strategy before cluster creation.
Use managed identity, Workload Identity, Key Vault integration, and policy controls instead of static secrets.
Review command impact, cost, and subscription context before running Azure CLI actions.
anti_patterns: Do not provision production clusters without confirming region, quota, IP space, and upgrade policy.
Do not expose credentials, tokens, subscription identifiers, or cluster kubeconfig in prompts or outputs.
Do not rely on B-series virtual machines or single-zone layouts for production workloads.

faq.question: Does this skill create AKS clusters automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and command examples. Users must approve and run any Azure actions.

faq.question: When should I choose AKS Automatic?
faq.answer: Choose AKS Automatic when you want managed defaults and do not need deep node pool customization.

faq.question: When should I choose AKS Standard?
faq.answer: Choose AKS Standard when you need more control over networking, autoscaling, node pools, or cluster configuration.

faq.question: Why are Day-0 decisions important?
faq.answer: Networking and API server choices can be hard to change after cluster creation. Plan them before provisioning.

faq.question: Can it help with AKS security?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers Entra ID, Workload Identity, Key Vault, Azure Policy, signed images, and Defender for Containers.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
